title: Marking gauge
subtitle: Setting and using the marking gauge for accurate parallel lines in wood joinery
excerpt: The marking gauge scribes a line parallel to a face or edge at a consistent distance. It is the first tool used in almost every hand-tool joinery operation: marking tenon thickness, mortise depth, dovetail baseline, and rebate width all begin with a marking gauge line. A line scribed with the grain is a clean groove; a line across the grain is a knife cut that prevents fibres from lifting during sawing. This beginner technique covers setting the fence, the two-handed push stroke, and grain-direction discipline.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for marking gauge use in joinery. Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for the marking gauge in mortise and tenon layout.

content.text: The
marking gauge

is the first tool in most hand-tool joinery. A scribed line is sharper than a pencil line. The pin cuts into the wood fibres. A saw blade guided to a scribed line splits the fibres cleanly along it. A pencil line just shows where the split should be. The
fence
rests against a reference face. As long as you hold the fence tight to that face, the line stays parallel to the face at the set distance, even if your push stroke wobbles.
What it does

The marking gauge scribes a line parallel to a reference face at a precisely set distance. It is used to mark tenon thickness, mortise width and depth, dovetail baseline, rebate depth, and any other measurement that must be consistently reproduced from a face or edge.
When to use it
At the start of every joinery sequence that involves cutting to a measurement from a face or edge. Not suitable for marking irregular shapes or curves; those require a pencil and a template.
What you need

A marking gauge with a sharp scribing pin. A try square to verify the fence is setting squarely to the stem. The scribing pin should be sharpened to a clean point before use; a blunt pin tears the fibres rather than cutting them, producing a ragged line that is hard to saw to accurately.
Safety
The scribing pin is sharp; store the gauge with the pin facing away from the hand. Keep fingers behind the fence, not in front of the pin.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Set the distance

Loosen the fence locking screw. Hold the gauge against a rule and slide the fence until the pin is at the target distance from the fence face. Tighten the screw. Test on a scrap piece: hold the fence against the face, push the gauge along the wood, and measure the resulting line with a rule. Adjust if needed; repeat the test until the measurement is exact.
Step 2: Register the fence firmly

Hold the fence flat against the reference face with the thumb and fingers of the gauging hand. The fence must not lift or rock during the stroke; a lifted fence produces a wandering line. The other hand holds the workpiece steady on the bench.
Step 3: The push stroke

Tilt the gauge slightly forward (in the direction of travel) so the pin drags behind the stem rather than digging in. Push along the grain with a smooth, even stroke. On with-grain strokes the pin scribes a clean line; on cross-grain strokes the pin cuts across the fibres and will tear if forced. For cross-grain lines, make several light passes rather than one heavy stroke, or scribe with the pin lightly initially and deepen with a second pass.
Step 4: Mark all faces before sawing

For a tenon, mark the thickness on both wide faces and both edges, always from the same reference face. This ensures that when the saw cuts to the line on each face, the resulting tenon is square even if the saw drifts slightly on any individual pass.
What it looks like when it is right

A clean V-groove in the wood surface, consistent in depth from end to end, exactly parallel to the reference face. The groove should be narrow enough that a saw blade placed against it registers without rocking.
Common mistakes
The fence lifts off the reference face mid-stroke: the line wanders. Slow down and concentrate on keeping the fence in contact throughout rather than on moving quickly.

The pin digs in on cross-grain strokes: the gauge is being pushed too hard. Use light, repeated strokes on cross-grain lines and tilt the gauge forward more than usual to keep the pin dragging rather than digging.
